summary: DHH announces the 2nd edition of 'Agile Web Development with Rails' (AWDwR), timed to coincide with the Rails 1.2 release. He praises publisher Pragmatic Bookshelf and author Dave Thomas for the extraordinary effort of keeping the book synchronized with Rails' rapid evolution. The book covers new features like migrations, polymorphic associations, respond_to, RJS, and resources. DHH highlights the extensive beta testing process and thanks first-edition buyers for helping put Rails on the map.
key_points: The 2nd edition of AWDwR is coordinated to ship alongside Rails 1.2, covering all major new features from the past year
Coordinating a book with a fast-moving open source project like Rails is extraordinarily difficult, requiring constant rewrites as the framework evolves
The book underwent months of beta testing by hundreds of developers, resulting in exceptional polish
The first edition's commercial success helped build the Rails ecosystem and economic momentum
The Pragmatic Bookshelf's innovative publishing model (PDF now, paper later) suited Rails' rapid pace
money_quotes: Coordinating a book with any open source project is notoriously difficult, coordinating one with Rails almost inhumanly so.
Regular book cycles can easily spend a year from idea to print. Considering the pace of evolution in the Rails world, that obviously wouldn't work.
Every time we reverted our opinion, change defaults, or introduced new API elements that deprecated old ones, Dave has been there to rewrite and update sections of the book to match.
This is truly the one book that every Rails developer and every aspiring Rails developer should have in their possession.
I'm really proud to see this level of documentation available for this little collection of Ruby scripts I started just over three years ago.
Its phenomenal success has really helped put Rails on the map.
key_insight: The rapid evolution of Rails demanded a radically different approach to technical publishing, and the Pragmatic Bookshelf's agile process proved that books could keep pace with fast-moving open source projects.
